(11) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Deploying Your First Blog
Time to deploy your first blog.
From here on we'll be using GitHub as the hosting for our website. You might be thinking:
"GitHub? Isn't that for source code? How does Hexo fit?"
GitHub actually has a feature called
that lets you host static project demo pages. For example, that random
Hexschool Ironman search tool (article in Mandarin)
I built one bored afternoon lives on gh-pages.
You might wonder if GitHub really allows using a repo as a blog. It does — GitHub Pages explicitly shows three categories, one of which is literally "Blogging with Jekyll":
So you can use GitHub Pages as free blog hosting. On top of that, GitHub Pages gives you free HTTPS, which is a big plus — if you self-host, you have to generate SSL certs yourself, which is a pain.
(Plenty of people use GitHub Pages as their blog host.)
So is GitHub Pages totally unrestricted? Not exactly. Here's the official wording, with quick gloss:

GitHub Pages source repositories have a recommended limit of 1GB. — Repo size capped at 1 GB.
Published GitHub Pages sites may be no larger than 1 GB. — Published site content can't exceed 1 GB.
GitHub Pages sites have a soft bandwidth limit of 100GB per month. — Soft cap of 100 GB bandwidth per month.
GitHub Pages sites have a soft limit of 10 builds per hour. — Soft cap of 10 builds per hour.
For a regular blog, these limits are way more than enough.
I'm assuming you already have a GitHub account. If not,
look at me doing free advertising for GitHub
Create a new repo (in case the GitHub UI has changed, here's the
. (This is critical — if you get it wrong, you won't be able to reach your blog at
Whatever you want. It's optional. I put "Hexo Blog".
Here I'm using one of my throwaway accounts as the demo:
Now install Hexo's deploy plugin. Hexo doesn't ship with one-click deploy built in, so you need to install it separately:
(heads up — this is Hexo's config, not the theme's
), and add this at the bottom:

deploy:
  type: git
  repo: https://github.com/hsianghexo/hsianghexo.github.io.git # https://github.com/[your-username]/[your-username].github.io.git
  branch: master # using master as the main branch here
  message: 'ithelpt'
# using master as the main branch here
You only really need to update
is up to you — fill in something or skip it entirely.
Last step: deploy Hexo to GitHub. Just run these in order:

Change it to the URL of the GitHub repo you just created. If the repo is
url: https://hsianghexo.github.io # be careful not to add a trailing "/", or you'll hit issues
# be careful not to add a trailing "/", or you'll hit issues
Now you can run the deploy commands. Typing those two long commands every time gets annoying, so you can use
as a shortcut — it generates static files and deploys in one go.
Branch 'master' set up to track remote branch 'master'
, the deploy is done. If you visit
in your browser and get a 404, that's because GitHub Pages isn't enabled yet (it usually auto-enables, but exceptions happen — next section covers manual enablement).
at the top of your repo:
Scroll down or search for "GitHub Pages", point
Refresh the page and confirm that "GitHub Pages" shows
— that means your blog is live. (If it says "still preparing", just give it a moment.)
GitHub usually has some caching, so it can take 5-15 minutes. If you keep getting 404, try deleting the
folder (usually hidden), then run these in order. That generally fixes it — most common on the first deploy:
This post ran a bit long, mainly because deploy trips up a lot of people. If I didn't spell it out...
